Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.title: Capacitance
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.content:
Capacitance
Capacitance is the ability of a system to store electric charge per unit voltage.
Definition
C = \frac{Q}{\Delta V}
Where:

Q is the magnitude of charge on each conductor
\Delta V is the potential difference between conductors

Units: Farads (F) = Coulombs/Volt (C/V)
Parallel Plate Capacitor
C = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}
Where:

A is the area of each plate
d is the separation between plates
\epsilon_0 is the permittivity of free space

Isolated Charged Conducting Sphere
C = 4\pi\epsilon_0 R
Where R is the radius of the sphere.
Spherical Capacitor
Two concentric spherical shells with radii a (inner) and b (outer):
C = 4\pi\epsilon_0 \frac{ab}{b-a}
Cylindrical Capacitor
Two concentric cylinders with radii a (inner) and b (outer) and length L:
C = \frac{2\pi\epsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)}
Energy Stored in a Capacitor
U = \frac{1}{2}\frac{Q^2}{C} = \frac{1}{2}Q\Delta V = \frac{1}{2}C(\Delta V)^2
Energy Density
For any electric field configuration:
u_E = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2

Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.filePath: Conductors, Capacitors, and Dielectrics/Combinations of Capacitors.md
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.title: Combinations of Capacitors
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.content:
Combinations of Capacitors
Capacitors can be combined in series and parallel to achieve desired capacitance values.
Capacitors in Parallel
For capacitors connected in parallel:

Same voltage across each capacitor: \Delta V = \Delta V_1 = \Delta V_2 = ...
Total charge: Q = Q_1 + Q_2 + ...
Equivalent capacitance: C_{\text{eq}} = C_1 + C_2 + ...

C_{\text{eq}} = \sum_i C_i

Capacitors in Series
For capacitors connected in series:

Same charge on each capacitor: Q = Q_1 = Q_2 = ...
Total voltage: \Delta V = \Delta V_1 + \Delta V_2 + ...
Equivalent capacitance: \frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + ...

\frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \sum_i \frac{1}{C_i}
For two capacitors in series:
C_{\text{eq}} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
Example: Three Capacitors
Two in parallel, then series with third:
If C_1 and C_2 are in parallel, and this combination is in series with C_3:

C_{12} = C_1 + C_2 (parallel)
C_{\text{eq}} = \frac{C_{12} C_3}{C_{12} + C_3} (series)

Voltage Division (Series)
For capacitors in series, the voltage divides inversely with capacitance:
\frac{\Delta V_1}{\Delta V_2} = \frac{C_2}{C_1}
Charge Division (Parallel)
For capacitors in parallel, the charge divides directly with capacitance:
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{C_1}{C_2}
Related Concepts

Capacitance
Electric Circuits
RC Circuits

Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.title: Dielectrics
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.content:
Dielectrics
A dielectric is an insulating material that increases the capacitance of a capacitor when inserted between the plates.
Dielectric Constant
The dielectric constant \kappa (kappa) is the ratio of capacitance with dielectric to capacitance without:
\kappa = \frac{C}{C_0}
Where C_0 is the capacitance with vacuum between plates.
Effect on Capacitance
C = \kappa C_0 = \kappa \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{\kappa \epsilon_0 A}{d}
Using the permittivity of the dielectric material \epsilon = \kappa \epsilon_0:
C = \frac{\epsilon A}{d}
Effect on Electric Field
The dielectric reduces the electric field between the plates:
E = \frac{E_0}{\kappa}
This occurs due to polarization of the dielectric material:

External field aligns molecular dipoles
Aligned dipoles create an opposing internal field
Net field is reduced

Effect on Voltage
For constant charge Q:
\Delta V = \frac{\Delta V_0}{\kappa}
Effect on Stored Energy
For constant charge (isolated capacitor):
U = \frac{U_0}{\kappa}
For constant voltage (connected to battery):
U = \kappa U_0
Dielectric Strength
The dielectric strength is the maximum electric field a material can withstand without breakdown.






























MaterialDielectric Constant \kappaDielectric Strength (MV/m)Air1.00063Paper3.716Glass5-1010-40Teflon2.160

Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.filePath: Conductors, Capacitors, and Dielectrics/Energy Stored in Capacitors.md
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.title: Energy Stored in Capacitors
Conductors,-Capacitors,-and-Dielectrics/.content:
Energy Stored in Capacitors
A capacitor stores energy in its electric field.
Energy Stored
U = \frac{1}{2}\frac{Q^2}{C} = \frac{1}{2}Q\Delta V = \frac{1}{2}C(\Delta V)^2
Where:

Q is the charge on each plate
C is the capacitance
\Delta V is the potential difference

Energy Density
The energy per unit volume stored in any electric field:
u_E = \frac{U}{\text{volume}} = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2
For a dielectric with permittivity \epsilon = \kappa \epsilon_0:
u_E = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon E^2 = \frac{1}{2}\kappa\epsilon_0 E^2
Total Energy in Electric Field
U = \int u_E \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 \int E^2 \, dV
This integral is taken over all space where the electric field exists.
Work Done Charging a Capacitor
The work required to charge a capacitor:
W = \int_0^Q V \, dq = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{2}\frac{Q^2}{C}
This work is stored as potential energy in the capacitor.
Example: Parallel Plate Capacitor
For a parallel plate capacitor with area A and separation d:
U = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}\left(\frac{Qd}{\epsilon_0 A}\right)^2 = \frac{Q^2 d}{2\epsilon_0 A}
Using E = \frac{Q}{\epsilon_0 A}:
U = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2 \cdot Ad
Which confirms the energy density formula.
Related Concepts

Capacitance
Electric Potential Energy
Electric Field

Differential-Equations-in-Mechanics.title: Differential Equations in Mechanics
Differential-Equations-in-Mechanics.content:
Differential Equations in Mechanics
Many problems in AP Physics C Mechanics involve solving differential equations to find position, velocity, or other quantities as functions of time. This note covers the two primary methods for solving first-order differential equations encountered in mechanics: separation of variables and integrating factors.
First-Order Linear Differential Equations
The standard form:
\frac{dy}{dt} + P(t)y = Q(t)
In mechanics, y often represents velocity v(t) or position x(t), and t is time.

Method 1: Separation of Variables
When to Use
Use when you can rewrite the equation so that all terms involving y are on one side and all terms involving t are on the other.
General Form
\frac{dy}{dt} = f(y) \cdot g(t)
Can be separated as:
\frac{dy}{f(y)} = g(t)\, dt
Steps

Rearrange: \frac{dy}{f(y)} = g(t)\, dt
Integrate both sides: \int \frac{dy}{f(y)} = \int g(t)\, dt
Solve for y(t)
Apply initial conditions to find constants

Example 1: Velocity with Linear Drag
A sphere falling through air with drag force F_d = -bv:
m\frac{dv}{dt} = mg - bv
Separating variables:
\frac{dv}{mg - bv} = \frac{dt}{m}
Integrating:
-\frac{1}{b}\ln|mg - bv| = \frac{t}{m} + C
With v(0) = 0:
v(t) = \frac{mg}{b}\left(1 - e^{-\frac{bt}{m}}\right)
As t \to \infty: v \to v_{terminal} = \frac{mg}{b}
Example 2: Radioactive Decay Analogy
The same mathematics applies to RC circuits and radioactive decay:
\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N \quad \Rightarrow \quad N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}

Method 2: Integrating Factors
When to Use
Use for first-order linear equations of the form:
\frac{dy}{dt} + P(t)y = Q(t)
When separation is difficult or impossible.
The Integrating Factor
\mu(t) = e^{\int P(t)\, dt}
Steps

Identify P(t) and Q(t)
Calculate integrating factor: \mu(t) = e^{\int P(t)\, dt}
Multiply both sides by \mu(t):
\mu(t)\frac{dy}{dt} + \mu(t)P(t)y = \mu(t)Q(t)
Left side becomes \frac{d}{dt}[\mu(t)y]:
\frac{d}{dt}[\mu(t)y] = \mu(t)Q(t)
Integrate both sides:
\mu(t)y = \int \mu(t)Q(t)\, dt + C
Solve for y:
y = \frac{1}{\mu(t)}\left(\int \mu(t)Q(t)\, dt + C\right)

Example: RL Circuit Analogy
In an RL circuit:
L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri = \mathcal{E}
Standard form:
\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{L}
Integrating factor:
\mu(t) = e^{\int \frac{R}{L} dt} = e^{\frac{Rt}{L}}
Solution:
i(t) = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-\frac{Rt}{L}}\right)

Comparison of Methods




















MethodBest ForKey StepSeparation of Variables\frac{dy}{dt} = f(y)g(t)Separate then integrateIntegrating FactorLinear: \frac{dy}{dt} + P(t)y = Q(t)Multiply by \mu(t) = e^{\int P dt}

Key Insights for Mechanics
Terminal Velocity
Many drag problems lead to solutions of the form:
v(t) = v_{\infty} + (v_0 - v_{\infty})e^{-t/\tau}
Where:

v_{\infty} = terminal velocity
\tau = time constant
v_0 = initial velocity

Time Constants
The characteristic time \tau = \frac{m}{b} (for linear drag) determines how quickly the system approaches terminal velocity.

After t = \tau: v is within 37\% of terminal
After t = 3\tau: v is within 5\% of terminal
After t = 5\tau: v is within 1\% of terminal


Common Mistakes to Avoid

Forgetting absolute values in logarithms: \ln|mg - bv|, not \ln(mg - bv)
Sign errors: Be consistent with direction (e.g., upward positive)
Initial conditions: Apply after finding general solution, not before integration
Units: Check that exponents are dimensionless (\frac{bt}{m} has units of 1)
Assuming separation always works: Some equations require integrating factors


Worked Examples
Example 1: Sphere with Air Resistance

File: Example 1 - Sphere with Air Resistance
Method: Separation of variables
Key Equation: \frac{dv}{dt} = -g - \frac{b}{m}v

Example 2: Box with Time-Varying Force

File: Example 2 - Box with Time-Varying Force
Method: Direct integration (special case where \frac{dv}{dt} = f(t))
Key Equation: \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{F_0}{M}e^{-Bt} - \mu_k g

Example 3: Stone in Water

File: Example 3 - Stone in Water
Method: Separation of variables for velocity-dependent drag
Key Equation: \frac{dv}{dt} = g - \frac{B}{m} - \frac{k}{m}v

Atwood Machine - Variable mass systems

Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.filePath: Electric Charges, Fields, and Gauss's Law/Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium.md
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.title: Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.content:
Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium
A conductor is a material in which charges are free to move. In electrostatic equilibrium, no net motion of charge occurs.
Properties of Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium


Electric field is zero inside the conductor
If \vec{E} \neq 0 inside, charges would move, violating equilibrium.


Excess charge resides entirely on the surface
Using Gauss’s law with a Gaussian surface inside the conductor:
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0} = 0


Electric field just outside the surface is perpendicular to the surface
E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}
If there were a tangential component, surface charges would move.


Charge density is greatest on regions of greatest curvature
For a conductor with radius of curvature R:
\sigma \propto \frac{1}{R}
Sharp points have high charge density and strong electric fields.


Hollow Conductors
For a conductor with a hollow cavity:

Electric field is zero in the cavity if no charge is inside
Any charge placed inside the cavity induces an equal and opposite charge on the inner surface
The outer surface charge adjusts to maintain E = 0 inside the conductor

Applications

Faraday cage: Shielding from external electric fields
Van de Graaff generator: Accumulation of charge on a conducting sphere
Electrostatic precipitator: Removing particles from gas streams

Related Concepts

Gauss’s Law
Electric Field
Electric Potential
Capacitance

Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.title: Electric Charge and Coulomb's Law
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.content:
Electric Charge and Coulomb’s Law
Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electromagnetic field.
Properties of Electric Charge


Quantization: Charge comes in discrete multiples of the elementary charge e
q = ne \quad \text{where } n = 0, \pm1, \pm2, ...
e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}


Conservation: The total electric charge in an isolated system remains constant


Two types: Positive (+) and negative (-) charges; like charges repel, opposite charges attract


Coulomb’s Law
The electric force between two point charges is:
\vec{F}_{12} = k_e \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{21}
Where:

k_e = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 8.99 \times 10^9 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}^2 (Coulomb’s constant)
\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2) (permittivity of free space)
r is the distance between charges
\hat{r}_{21} is the unit vector from q_1 toward q_2

Superposition Principle
For multiple charges, the net force on a charge is the vector sum of individual forces:
\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = \sum_i \vec{F}_i = k_e q \sum_i \frac{q_i}{r_i^2} \hat{r}_i
Related Concepts

Electric Field
Electric Potential
Gauss’s Law

Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.filePath: Electric Charges, Fields, and Gauss's Law/Electric Field.md
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.title: Electric Field
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.content:
Electric Field
The electric field \vec{E} is a vector field that describes the force per unit charge experienced by a test charge at any point in space.
Definition
\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}
Where \vec{F} is the electric force on a small test charge q_0.
Electric Field of a Point Charge
\vec{E} = k_e \frac{q}{r^2} \hat{r}
For a positive charge, the field points radially outward; for a negative charge, it points radially inward.
Electric Field Lines

Origin on positive charges, terminate on negative charges
Never cross each other
Density of lines indicates field strength
Tangent to field line gives direction of \vec{E}

Electric Field of Continuous Charge Distributions
For a continuous charge distribution:
\vec{E} = k_e \int \frac{dq}{r^2} \hat{r}
Common distributions:
Line charge (linear charge density \lambda):
dq = \lambda \, dl
Surface charge (surface charge density \sigma):
dq = \sigma \, dA
Volume charge (volume charge density \rho):
dq = \rho \, dV
Example: Ring of Charge
For a uniformly charged ring of radius R and total charge Q:
On the axis (distance x from center):
E_x = \frac{k_e Qx}{(x^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}
At the center (x = 0):
E = 0
Far from the ring (x \gg R):
E \approx \frac{k_e Q}{x^2}

Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.filePath: Electric Charges, Fields, and Gauss's Law/Gauss's Law.md
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.title: Gauss's Law
Electric-Charges,-Fields,-and-Gauss's-La.content:
Gauss’s Law
Gauss’s Law relates the electric flux through a closed surface to the enclosed charge. It is one of Maxwell’s four equations.
Electric Flux
Electric flux through a surface is defined as:
\Phi_E = \int_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A}
For a closed surface (Gaussian surface):
\Phi_E = \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A}
Gauss’s Law Statement
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0}
Where:

\Phi_E is the electric flux through the closed surface
Q_{\text{enc}} is the total charge enclosed by the surface
\epsilon_0 is the permittivity of free space

Applications to Symmetric Charge Distributions
1. Spherical Symmetry (Point Charge, Charged Sphere)
For a spherical Gaussian surface of radius r:
E \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0}
E = \frac{k_e Q_{\text{enc}}}{r^2}
Solid sphere of radius R with uniform charge density \rho:

Inside (r < R): E = \frac{k_e Q r}{R^3}
Outside (r > R): E = \frac{k_e Q}{r^2}

Spherical shell of radius R:

Inside (r < R): E = 0
Outside (r > R): E = \frac{k_e Q}{r^2}

2. Cylindrical Symmetry (Line Charge, Charged Cylinder)
For a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length L:
E \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0}
E = \frac{2k_e \lambda}{r}
Where \lambda is the linear charge density.
3. Planar Symmetry (Infinite Sheet)
For an infinite sheet with surface charge density \sigma:
E = \frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}
The field is uniform and perpendicular to the sheet.
Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium

Electric field is zero inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium
Excess charge resides entirely on the surface
Electric field just outside the surface is perpendicular to the surface:
E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}
Charge accumulates on regions of greatest curvature (sharp points)

Related Concepts

Electric-Circuits/Current-and-Resistance.title: Current and Resistance
Electric-Circuits/Current-and-Resistance.content:
Current and Resistance
Electric Current
Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge.
I = \frac{dq}{dt}
Units: Amperes (A) = Coulombs/second (C/s)
Current Density
\vec{J} = nq\vec{v}_d
Where:

n is the charge carrier density (number per unit volume)
q is the charge per carrier
\vec{v}_d is the drift velocity

For a wire with cross-sectional area A:
I = JA = nqv_d A
Resistance and Ohm’s Law
Resistance
R = \frac{\Delta V}{I}
Units: Ohms (\Omega) = Volts/Ampere (V/A)
Ohm’s Law
For ohmic materials:
\Delta V = IR
Resistivity
R = \rho \frac{L}{A}
Where:

\rho is the resistivity of the material
L is the length of the conductor
A is the cross-sectional area

Temperature dependence:
\rho = \rho_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]
Where \alpha is the temperature coefficient of resistivity.
Power in Electric Circuits
P = I\Delta V = I^2R = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{R}
Energy Dissipated (Joule Heating)
E = Pt = I^2Rt
Drift Velocity
v_d = \frac{I}{nqA}
Example: For copper wire with n \approx 8.5 \times 10^{28} electrons/m³ carrying 1 A in a 1 mm² wire:
v_d \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}

Electric-Circuits/DC-Circuits.title: DC Circuits
Electric-Circuits/DC-Circuits.content:
DC Circuits
Direct current (DC) circuits contain sources of constant voltage and resistors connected in various configurations.
Resistors in Series

Same current through each resistor
Total voltage: \Delta V = \Delta V_1 + \Delta V_2 + ...
Equivalent resistance: R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + ...

R_{\text{eq}} = \sum_i R_i
Resistors in Parallel

Same voltage across each resistor
Total current: I = I_1 + I_2 + ...
Equivalent resistance: \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + ...

\frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \sum_i \frac{1}{R_i}
For two resistors in parallel:
R_{\text{eq}} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
Kirchhoff’s Rules
Junction Rule (Kirchhoff’s Current Law)
The sum of currents entering a junction equals the sum of currents leaving:
\sum I_{\text{in}} = \sum I_{\text{out}}
This is a statement of charge conservation.
Loop Rule (Kirchhoff’s Voltage Law)
The sum of potential differences around any closed loop is zero:
\sum \Delta V = 0
Sign conventions:

Voltage gain (+) when going from - to + across a battery
Voltage drop (-) when going through a resistor in direction of current
Voltage drop (-) when going from + to - across a battery

Example: Single Loop Circuit
For a circuit with battery V and resistors R_1, R_2, R_3 in series:
I = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}
Example: Two-Loop Circuit
Using Kirchhoff’s rules for a circuit with two loops:

Apply junction rule at the junction
Apply loop rule to each loop
Solve the system of equations

Related Concepts

Current and Resistance
RC Circuits

Electric-Circuits/Power-in-Electric-Circ.title: Power in Electric Circuits
Electric-Circuits/Power-in-Electric-Circ.content:
Power in Electric Circuits
Power Delivered by a Battery
P = I\Delta V = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{R_{\text{eq}}}
Where \Delta V is the emf of the battery and R_{\text{eq}} is the equivalent resistance.
Power Dissipated by a Resistor
P = I\Delta V = I^2R = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{R}
This is the rate at which electrical energy is converted to thermal energy (Joule heating).
Energy Dissipated
E = Pt = I^2Rt
Power in Series Circuits
For resistors R_1, R_2, ... in series with current I:
P_{\text{total}} = I^2(R_1 + R_2 + ...) = \sum P_i
Power in Parallel Circuits
For resistors in parallel with voltage \Delta V:
P_{\text{total}} = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \sum \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{R_i} = \sum P_i
Maximum Power Transfer
Maximum power is delivered to the load when:
R_{\text{load}} = R_{\text{internal}}
The maximum power is:
P_{\text{max}} = \frac{\mathcal{E}^2}{4R_{\text{internal}}}
Where \mathcal{E} is the emf of the source.
Example: Light bulb Power
A 60 W, 120 V light bulb:
R = \frac{V^2}{P} = \frac{120^2}{60} = 240\ \Omega
I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5\ \text{A}

Electric-Circuits/RC-Circuits.title: RC Circuits
Electric-Circuits/RC-Circuits.content:
RC Circuits
An RC circuit contains a resistor and capacitor connected to a voltage source or to each other.
Charging a Capacitor
When a capacitor charges through a resistor:
Charge as a function of time
q(t) = Q_f\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
Current as a function of time
I(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau} = \frac{V}{R} e^{-t/\tau}
Where:

Q_f = CV is the final charge on the capacitor
I_0 = V/R is the initial current
\tau = RC is the time constant

Time Constant
\tau = RC
The time constant represents:

Time to reach ~63.2% of final charge
Time for current to decrease to ~36.8% of initial value

Voltage across capacitor
\Delta V_C(t) = V\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
Voltage across resistor
\Delta V_R(t) = V e^{-t/\tau}
Discharging a Capacitor
When a charged capacitor discharges through a resistor:
Charge as a function of time
q(t) = Q_0 e^{-t/\tau}
Current as a function of time
I(t) = -I_0 e^{-t/\tau} = -\frac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/\tau}
(Negative sign indicates opposite direction)
Voltage across capacitor
\Delta V_C(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}
Energy Considerations
Energy stored in capacitor at any time:
U(t) = \frac{q(t)^2}{2C}
Energy dissipated by resistor during charging:
U_R = \frac{1}{2}CV^2
(Equal to energy stored in capacitor at full charge)

Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential-En.title: Electric Potential Energy
Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential-En.content:
Electric Potential Energy
Electric potential energy U is the energy associated with the configuration of charges in an electric field.
Work Done by Electric Field
The work done by the electric field on a charge q moving from point A to B:
W_{AB} = \int_A^B \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l} = q \int_A^B \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}
Change in Potential Energy
\Delta U = U_B - U_A = -W_{AB} = -q \int_A^B \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}
Electric Potential Energy of Point Charges
Two point charges q_1 and q_2 separated by distance r:
U = k_e \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}
System of multiple point charges:
U_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i<j} k_e \frac{q_i q_j}{r_{ij}}
Reference Point
Electric potential energy is defined relative to a reference point where U = 0. Typically chosen as:

Infinity for isolated point charges
Ground for circuits

Potential Energy in Constant Electric Field
For a charge q moving a distance d in a uniform field \vec{E}:
\Delta U = -qEd \cos\theta = -q\vec{E} \cdot \vec{d}
Where \theta is the angle between \vec{E} and the displacement.
Relationship to Electric Potential
U = qV
Where V is the electric potential.
Related Concepts

Electric Potential
Electric Charge and Coulomb’s Law
Conservation of Energy

Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential-of.title: Electric Potential of Conductors
Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential-of.content:
Electric Potential of Conductors
In electrostatic equilibrium, conductors have special properties regarding their electric potential.
Key Properties


The entire conductor is at the same potential (equipotential)
Since \vec{E} = 0 inside, \Delta V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0


Surface is an equipotential surface
No work is required to move charge along the surface


Potential of a Charged Conducting Sphere
Solid sphere of radius R with total charge Q:


Outside (r \geq R):
V = k_e \frac{Q}{r}


Inside (r < R):
V = k_e \frac{Q}{R} = \text{constant}


At the surface:
V = k_e \frac{Q}{R}


Potential of a Spherical Shell
Thin shell of radius R with total charge Q:


Outside (r \geq R):
V = k_e \frac{Q}{r}


Inside (r < R):
V = k_e \frac{Q}{R} = \text{constant}


Hollow Conductors
For a conductor with a hollow cavity:

If no charge in cavity: Entire conductor (including cavity) is equipotential
If charge +q in cavity: Inner surface acquires -q, outer surface acquires +q

Outer surface potential: V = k_e \frac{q}{R}



Capacitors and Conducting Plates
Parallel plate capacitor with surface charge densities +\sigma and -\sigma:

Field between plates: E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}
Potential difference: \Delta V = Ed = \frac{\sigma d}{\epsilon_0}
Each plate is an equipotential surface

Related Concepts

Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium
Electric Potential
Capacitance
Gauss’s Law

Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential.title: Electric Potential
Electric-Potential/Electric-Potential.content:
Electric Potential
Electric potential V is the electric potential energy per unit charge. It’s a scalar field that describes the potential energy of a charge at any point in space.
Definition
V = \frac{U}{q} = -\int_{\text{ref}}^P \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}
Units: Volts (V) = Joules/Coulomb (J/C)
Electric Potential Difference
\Delta V = V_B - V_A = -\int_A^B \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}
Electric Potential Due to Point Charges
For a point charge q at distance r (with V = 0 at \infty):
V = k_e \frac{q}{r}
For multiple point charges:
V = k_e \sum_i \frac{q_i}{r_i}
For continuous charge distribution:
V = k_e \int \frac{dq}{r}
Relationship Between Electric Field and Potential
\vec{E} = -\nabla V
In Cartesian coordinates:
E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}
Equipotential Surfaces

Surfaces of constant potential (V = \text{constant})
Electric field lines are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces
No work is required to move a charge along an equipotential surface

Examples
Ring of Charge (Radius R, Total Charge Q)
On the axis (distance x from center):
V = \frac{k_e Q}{\sqrt{x^2 + R^2}}
Charged Disk (Radius R, Surface Charge \sigma)
On the axis (distance x from center):
V = \frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}\left(\sqrt{x^2 + R^2} - |x|\right)
Infinite Charged Plane (Surface Charge \sigma)
V = -\frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0}|x|
Related Concepts

Electric Potential Energy
Electric Field
Electric Charge and Coulomb’s Law
Gauss’s Law

Electric-Potential/Relationship-Between-.filePath: Electric Potential/Relationship Between E and V.md
Electric-Potential/Relationship-Between-.title: Relationship Between E and V
Electric-Potential/Relationship-Between-.content:
Relationship Between E and V
The electric field and electric potential are fundamentally related through calculus operations.
Gradient Relationship
\vec{E} = -\nabla V
The electric field points in the direction of decreasing potential (steepest descent).
In Cartesian Coordinates
E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}
\vec{E} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right)
In Cylindrical Coordinates
E_r = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}, \quad E_\phi = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}, \quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}
In Spherical Coordinates
E_r = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}, \quad E_\theta = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}, \quad E_\phi = -\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}
Line Integral Relationship
\Delta V = V_B - V_A = -\int_A^B \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}
For a uniform electric field:
\Delta V = -Ed
Where d is the displacement in the direction of the field.
Conservative Nature
The electric field is conservative, meaning:

The work done is path-independent
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0 (closed loop integral is zero)
A scalar potential function V exists

Example: Point Charge
Given V = k_e \frac{q}{r}:
E_r = -\frac{dV}{dr} = -\frac{d}{dr}\left(k_e \frac{q}{r}\right) = \frac{k_e q}{r^2}
This recovers Coulomb’s law for the electric field.
Example: Uniform Field (Parallel Plates)
For \vec{E} = E_0 \hat{i}:
V(x) = -E_0 x + \text{constant}
The potential decreases linearly with distance in the direction of the field.
Related Concepts

Electric Field
Electric Potential
Electric Potential Energy

Electromagnetic-Induction/Faraday's-Law.title: Faraday's Law
Electromagnetic-Induction/Faraday's-Law.content:
Faraday’s Law
Faraday’s Law describes how a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force (emf).
Faraday’s Law of Induction
\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}
Where:

\mathcal{E} is the induced emf
\Phi_B is the magnetic flux through the circuit
The negative sign represents Lenz’s Law

Lenz’s Law
The induced emf and induced current are in such a direction as to oppose the change that produces them.
“Nature abhors a change in flux.”
Applications
Motional EMF
For a conducting rod of length L moving with velocity v perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field B:
\mathcal{E} = BLv
Direction: Given by the right-hand rule (charges experience force q\vec{v} \times \vec{B})
Rotating Loop
For a loop of area A rotating with angular velocity \omega in a uniform magnetic field B:
\mathcal{E} = NBA\omega\sin(\omega t)
This is the principle behind electric generators.
Solenoid (Mutual Inductance)
When the current in a primary coil changes, it induces an emf in a secondary coil:
\mathcal{E}_2 = -M\frac{dI_1}{dt}
Where M is the mutual inductance.
Self-Inductance
A changing current in a coil induces an emf in the same coil:
\mathcal{E} = -L\frac{dI}{dt}
Where L is the inductance.
Energy Considerations
The work done against the induced emf is stored as magnetic potential energy:
U = \frac{1}{2}LI^2
Maxwell’s Correction
Ampère’s law was incomplete. Maxwell added the displacement current term:
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}} + \mu_0\epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}
This correction shows that a changing electric field also creates a magnetic field.
Related Concepts

Magnetic Flux
Lenz’s Law
Inductance
Electromagnetic Induction
Maxwell’s Equations

Electromagnetic-Induction/Inductance.title: Inductance
Electromagnetic-Induction/Inductance.content:
Inductance
Inductance is the property of an electrical conductor that opposes changes in current.
Self-Inductance
When the current in a coil changes, the changing magnetic flux induces an emf that opposes the change:
\mathcal{E} = -L\frac{dI}{dt}
Where L is the inductance.
Units: Henry (H) = V·s/A = Wb/A
Definition of Inductance
L = \frac{N\Phi_B}{I}
Where:

N is the number of turns
\Phi_B is the magnetic flux through each turn
I is the current

Inductance of Common Configurations
Solenoid
L = \mu_0 n^2 A \ell = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}
Where:

n = N/\ell is the number of turns per unit length
A is the cross-sectional area
\ell is the length of the solenoid

Toroid
L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 h}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
Where h is the height and a, b are the inner and outer radii.
Energy Stored in an Inductor
U = \frac{1}{2}LI^2
Using the magnetic field energy density:
u_B = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
Total energy:
U = \int u_B \, dV = \frac{1}{2\mu_0} \int B^2 \, dV
RL Circuits
Current Growth (Switch Closed)
I(t) = \frac{V}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
Current Decay (Switch Opened)
I(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}
Where the inductive time constant is:
\tau = \frac{L}{R}
Mutual Inductance
When two coils are coupled:
\mathcal{E}_2 = -M\frac{dI_1}{dt}
Where M is the mutual inductance.
Related Concepts

Energy Stored in Inductors

Electromagnetic-Induction/LC-Circuits.title: LC Circuits
Electromagnetic-Induction/LC-Circuits.content:
LC Circuits
An LC circuit contains an inductor and capacitor connected together, creating an oscillating system.
Differential Equation
Applying Kirchhoff’s loop rule:
L\frac{d^2q}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{C}q = 0
This is the equation of a simple harmonic oscillator.
Charge as a Function of Time
q(t) = Q_{\text{max}}\cos(\omega t + \phi)
Where:

Q_{\text{max}} is the maximum charge on the capacitor
\phi is the phase constant (determined by initial conditions)

Angular Frequency
\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
Period:
T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}
Frequency:
f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
Current as a Function of Time
I(t) = -\frac{dq}{dt} = \omega Q_{\text{max}}\sin(\omega t + \phi)
I(t) = I_{\text{max}}\sin(\omega t + \phi)
Where I_{\text{max}} = \omega Q_{\text{max}}
Energy in an LC Circuit
Energy stored in capacitor:
U_E = \frac{q^2}{2C} = \frac{Q_{\text{max}}^2}{2C}\cos^2(\omega t + \phi)
Energy stored in inductor:
U_B = \frac{1}{2}LI^2 = \frac{LI_{\text{max}}^2}{2}\sin^2(\omega t + \phi)
Total energy (constant):
U_{\text{total}} = U_E + U_B = \frac{Q_{\text{max}}^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2}LI_{\text{max}}^2
Energy Oscillation
Energy continuously transfers between the electric field of the capacitor and the magnetic field of the inductor:

At q = \pm Q_{\text{max}}: All energy is electric (U_B = 0)
At q = 0: All energy is magnetic (U_E = 0)

Analogy to Mechanical Systems

































ElectricalMechanicalCharge qPosition xCurrent I = dq/dtVelocity v = dx/dtInductance LMass mInverse capacitance 1/CSpring constant kEnergy LI^2/2Kinetic energy mv^2/2Energy q^2/2CPotential energy kx^2/2

Electromagnetic-Induction/Magnetic-Flux.title: Magnetic Flux
Electromagnetic-Induction/Magnetic-Flux.content:
Magnetic Flux
Magnetic flux \Phi_B measures the amount of magnetic field passing through a surface.
Definition
\Phi_B = \int_S \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A}
For a uniform magnetic field and flat surface:
\Phi_B = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = BA\cos\theta
Where:

\vec{B} is the magnetic field
\vec{A} is the area vector (perpendicular to surface, magnitude = area)
\theta is the angle between \vec{B} and \vec{A}

Units: Weber (Wb) = T·m²
Gauss’s Law for Magnetism
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0
The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero, indicating that magnetic monopoles do not exist.
Examples
Uniform Field Perpendicular to Surface
\Phi_B = BA
Uniform Field at Angle \theta
\Phi_B = BA\cos\theta
Solenoid
For a solenoid with n turns per unit length, current I, cross-sectional area A:
\Phi_B = BA = (\mu_0 n I)A
For a coil with N turns:
\Phi_B = NBA = N\mu_0 n IA
Rotating Loop in Uniform Field
For a loop rotating with angular velocity \omega:
\Phi_B(t) = BA\cos(\omega t)
Relationship to Induced EMF
According to Faraday’s Law, a changing magnetic flux induces an emf:
\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}
Related Concepts

Faraday’s Law
Magnetic Field
Electromagnetic Induction

Electromagnetic-Induction/Maxwell's-Equa.title: Maxwell's Equations
Electromagnetic-Induction/Maxwell's-Equa.content:
Maxwell’s Equations
Maxwell’s Equations are a set of four fundamental equations that describe all classical electromagnetic phenomena.
The Four Equations (Integral Form)
1. Gauss’s Law for Electric Fields
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0}
Electric field lines originate on positive charges and terminate on negative charges.
2. Gauss’s Law for Magnetic Fields
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0
There are no magnetic monopoles; magnetic field lines form closed loops.
3. Faraday’s Law of Induction
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}
A changing magnetic flux creates an electric field.
4. Ampère-Maxwell Law
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}} + \mu_0\epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}
Magnetic fields are created by currents and by changing electric fields.
Maxwell’s Correction
The displacement current term \mu_0\epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt} was Maxwell’s crucial addition:

A changing electric field creates a magnetic field
This explains electromagnetic wave propagation
Predicts that electromagnetic waves travel at speed c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}}

Differential Form

Gauss’s Law: \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\epsilon_0}
Gauss’s Law for Magnetism: \nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0
Faraday’s Law: \nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
Ampère-Maxwell Law: \nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0\vec{J} + \mu_0\epsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}

Electromagnetic Waves
In vacuum (no charges or currents):
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0\epsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}
These equations describe electromagnetic waves traveling at:
c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}} \approx 3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}
Significance

Unified electricity, magnetism, and optics
Predicted electromagnetic waves before they were observed
Foundation for all classical electromagnetic theory
Led to special relativity

Related Concepts

Gauss’s Law
Faraday’s Law
Ampère’s Law
Magnetic Flux

Energy/Conservation-of-Energy.title: Conservation of Energy
Energy/Conservation-of-Energy.content:
Conservation of Energy
Conservation of Energy is one of the fundamental conservation laws in mechanics. In AP Physics C, it needs to be applied to systems that include rotation.
Statement
The total energy of an isolated system remains constant:
E_{total} = \text{constant}
or
\Delta E_{total} = 0
When only conservative forces do work, mechanical energy is conserved:
K + U = \text{constant}
Mechanical Energy with Rotation
For rotating systems, total mechanical energy includes rotational kinetic energy:
E = K_{trans} + K_{rot} + U

K_{trans} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{CM}^2 translational kinetic energy of the center of mass
K_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 rotational kinetic energy
U potential energy (gravitational, elastic, gravitational potential, etc.)

Application in Celestial Mechanics
In satellite orbital transfer, conservation of mechanical energy applies:
E_1 = E_2
-\frac{GMm}{2r_1} = -\frac{GMm}{2r_2} + W_{\text{engine}}
This can be used to calculate the energy required for orbital maneuvering.
Worked Examples

Example 3 - Stone in Water - Energy conservation with drag forces
Example 6 - Sliding Rope - Work-energy theorem with variable mass
Example 7 - Ball in Tunnel Through Planet - Gravitational potential energy inside uniform sphere
Example 8 - Power from Position Function - Power and kinetic energy calculations
Example 9 - Potential Energy and Turning Points - Energy conservation and bound states
Example 10 - Spring-Launch into Semicircular Track - Spring potential energy and energy conservation
Example 11 - Lennard-Jones Potential - Potential energy analysis and molecular dynamics
Example 19 - Ballistic Measurement with Spring - Collision, momentum, and spring potential energy

Related Concepts

Conservation of Rotational Energy
Gravitational Potential Energy
Satellite Orbital Motion
